title: Quantum-inspired Topographic Stereovision
abstract: We challenge the long-unquestioned triangulation in distant stereovision, where shape rather than distance is the relevant observable. Our information-regret analysis reveals that the optimal measurements for absolute distance and distance gradient are unexpectedly different and incompatible. To resolve this observable-measurement mismatch, we introduce stereo regularization to address stereo anisotropies that violate prevailing emitter-number conservation, and propose the topographic interferometer, which exploits cross-detector correlations to probe topography without measuring the distance profile. Our interferometer turns parallaxing paths into Mach-Zehnder arms and incorporates a central path as the local oscillator for balanced homodyne detection, saturating the quantum Fisher information with improved topographic error scaling. Our work enables topographic stereovision of thermal sources beyond the Rayleigh limit, thereby establishing a quantum-inspired framework for heat-assisted detection and ranging in remote sensing and astronomy.
summary: This work re-evaluates stereovision, arguing that topography is more fundamental than distance for distant objects. The authors propose a quantum-inspired topographic interferometer that uses quantum correlations to measure topography with precision surpassing classical limits. This establishes a new quantum framework for heat-assisted detection in fields like astronomy.
main_problem: The paper challenges the traditional reliance on triangulation for stereovision, arguing that for distant objects, topography is the more relevant observable than absolute distance.
main_result: The proposed topographic interferometer enables quantum-enhanced stereovision of thermal sources beyond the Rayleigh limit by exploiting cross-detector correlations.
method: It introduces stereo regularization and proposes a topographic interferometer that uses Mach-Zehnder arms and balanced homodyne detection to probe topography.
model_or_system: The system models the optical field from a thermal source in the X-Z plane, analyzing the superposition of photons across stereo spatial modes (L, R) fed into a beam splitter.
key_observables: Topography ($	heta$), absolute distance ($Z$), disparity ($\delta_q$), and the quantum Fisher information ($K_h$).
important_parameters: The analysis focuses on the long-range and weak-source limits ($\epsilon \ll 1$) and the incompatibility quantified by $\Delta^2 Z + \Delta^2 	heta \ge 	ext{constant}$.
main_assumptions: The method assumes the sub-Rayleigh regime and addresses stereo anisotropies violating emitter-number conservation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares traditional stereovision (measuring Z) versus topographic stereovision.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the observable-measurement mismatch, then introduces stereo regularization, and finally details the construction and analysis of the topographic interferometer to achieve quantum-enhanced ranging.
why_interesting: It applies advanced quantum estimation theory (Quantum Fisher Information) to a complex, non-equilibrium sensing problem (thermal imaging), providing a quantum-enhanced framework for remote sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quantum-inspired topographic stereovision with to- pographic interferometer (TI, see Fig. 2). Cross-detector correlations encode unique topographic information beyond triangulation, trading distance precision for improved topo- graphic error scaling. ∆: information regret. u: image plane coordinate. φu: modal profile.

FIG. 2. Topographic interferometer. A thermal photon emitted by the light source (black line segment) propagates (dashed blue line) to the apparatus and is in a superposition across stereo spatial modes (L: left, R: right), which are fed into the bottom beam splitter (BS) for interference. This pro- cess resembles a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, in which the contrast between two spatial modes encodes the topography of the thermal light source. We introduce a central spatial mode (C) as the local oscillator (LO) for balanced homodyne detection (BHD) to fully extract the topographic information. M: mirror. P: variable phase retarder. Q: quarter-wave phase retarder. Arrows with varying lengths:...
